Re: Conflict of Interest of a Rent Review
Commission - City of Palm Springs
Dear Mr. Garcia:
In furtherance of the letter we sent you May 3, 1990,
this l-etter is in response to the oral advice we under-
stanC you issued to the Assistant City Attorney of palm
Springs, Siegfried H. Siefkes.
STATEMENT OF FACTS
Our client, 
owner of the E1 Dorado Mobile Home park,
filed 
an Application in july of 1989 with the palm
springs Rent Revievr commission f or a hardship rent i-n-
crease.
Hearings began on the Apprication l-ast February. one of
the commissioners, who has attended meetj_ngs and partici-
pated in the Hearings since February, iJ married to a
real estate broker who acquired a risting for the sale
of a m.obile horne in the El- Dorado Mobile Home park.
Arguably, the commission's deci-sion on a rent increase
courd affect the amount of conm.ission the commissioner,s
husband wilr earn. our client,s financial interest may
be in conflict with the commissioner. However, our client
seeks to resorve this matter efficientry 
and has waived
any financial confricts he has with the commissioner.

that your initial 
thoughts are:

does not' create a conflict of interest, 
even
though the decision of the Comrnission could.
significantly 
affect the sale price of the
property listed, and thus the corunission whj-ch
the Commissioner's husband would receive.
If 
a prospective buyer is procured for the
listed 
n.obile home, a conflict 
of interest

procured if the prospective buyer makes a de-
posit towards the purchase of the mobile home.
If a conflict of interest does exist, counsel
for the mobile home owner petitioner and coun-
se1 for the tenants as real parties in interest
cannot waive that conf]ict 
of interest 
and
that Commissioner rnay not contj-nue to partici-
pate in the hearing. "
OUR POSITION
If 
a buyer is procured before the decisj-on on the
hardshlp rent increase Application, then the prj-ce of
the mobile home is fixeC. The Commissioner's vtte wilL
not affect the commi-ssion her husband receives. There is
no conflict since there is already an agreed upon price.
Furthermore, the Commissioner is not disqualifj-ed under
Callfornia Code of Regufations, Title 9, Section g7I03;
since her financial interest is not distinguishable from
the general public.
The FPPC has determined that the rental property industry
and tenants constitute the general public- witi respeci
to decisions implementing rent contiol measures. In re
Overstreet (1981) I FppC Ops. 7I.
In our analgous case, the tenants and mobile home park
comprise the general public as they did in In' re
overstreet. The effect of the Rent Rdview c"m*isffi
ToTE-;;--he hardship rent increase r,ritL not affect the
2.

Joseph Garc j- a, Esq.
June 22, ).990
Page Three
tenants, or the mobile home park in a manner distj"nguish-
able from the Commissioner; either both parties benefit
or neither benefits.
1f the Comrnissioner votes against the hardship rent
increase, her husband could receive a Iarger commission.
However, that commission pales in comparison to the
savings four hundred nobile home park tenants receive
because of rent control . The tenants gain any advantages
the Commissioner might indirectly 
receive. Because the
general public benefits with the Commissioner, there is
no conflict 
between them. Thus, the "general public"
exception appJ-ies to the Commissioner's financial- in-
terests.
The F'PPC has also aflowed Council members to participate
in a vote on enacting a rent control ordinance even
though they owned rental units in the targeted area. In
re Ferraro (1978) 4 FPPC Ops. 62
In that case, the Council members had a direct conffict
of interests with the tenants in the rent control area;
if the ordinance passed, the tenants benefited. If the
Council voted against the ordinance, then the Council
members who owned rental units profited by raising rents.
However, the FPPC did not find the Council- members'
financial- j-nterest distinguishable from that of the gen-
eral- public's because each Council nember owned less
than three renta] units.
Unlike the parties in In re Ferraro, the tenants and
Commissioner in our c a sE-liE-T6E--li 
adversarial posi-
tions. 
Both parties improve financiallly,

does. The Commissioner,s financiat interest in our case
clearly falls within the ,'general public,' exception of
Regulation 87103.
The only adverse party to the Commissioner is our client,
who has the fargest financial interest in the matter; ii
the tenants and Commissj-oner benefit, then our client
suffers because he cannot increase the rent.


LAW OFFICES
SHAPIRo, PoSELL & CLoSE
Joseph carc j.a, Esq.
June 22, 1990
Page Four
Because the Commissioner has invested countl,ess hours on
this matter since the proceedings began in I'ebruary, the
Commissioner's continued participation would be in the
Rent Review Commission's best interest. The five member
Commission would suffer if the Commi-ssioner could not
contribute her exper.ience and expertise in this matter.
Regulation 81003 states that the Political 
Reform Act
should be "liberal1y construed to accomplish its pur-
pose". The Act purports to eliminate various political-
i-mproprieties. This public policy consideration does not
apply to our case. The spirit of the Act .is not tainted
because complete information has been discl-osed to the
interested parties. Because our cfient is the only ad-
verse party to the Commissioner's financial .interest,
his waj.ver wou.Id efficiently 
resolve this matter.
Siegfried H. Siefkes, Assistant City Attorney for Palm
Springs, informed our client that the Rent Review Commis-
sion is conducting a "quasi-judicial 
proceeding". In
quasi-judicial hearings, parties are allowed to waive
confli-cts of interest. California Code of Civil Procedure
Section I70.3 al1ows for parties to waive the disqualifi-
cation of judges.
The same policy should apply to loca1 commissioners. If
the interested parties have knowledge of the conflict
and waive it, then the judge or commissioner can perform
thei-r duties.
Since the Commissioner does not have a financial interest
distinguishable from the general public, it 
would be
efficient 
and fair to aIlow the Conrnissioner to finish
participating in this matter.
Thank you for your consideration of this matter. If you
lave any questions, or are in need of any furtier
information, please do not hesj-tate to ca]t. 
We look
forward to hearing from you shortly.
Very truly yours,

Dear Mr. carcia:
This is a request for confirmation of the
given to me via telephone concerning the
situation.
advise which you have
following fact
*@

t
€a
The City of Pal-m Springs has a Rent Review Commission, the
members of which are appointed by the City Council. The duties
of this Commission incl-ude hearing and rnaking decisions on
petitions for hardship rent increases which are brought by owners
of residential rental properties.
The Cornmission is now conducting hearings on a petition for
hardship rent increase which was filed by the owner of a major
nobile home park l"ocated here in the cj.ty.
The husband of one of the Commissi-oners is a real estate broker.
The Cornrnissioners l:usban<i has a listing for the sale of one of
the nobile homes Located in the mobile home park which is the
subject of this hearing. If the rnobile home were sold for the
listed price, the Conmissioners husband would receive a
commission of $660. ?he decision of the Commission could have a
significant effect on the sale price of the rnobire horne. counsel
for the petitioning rnobile home park owner and counsel for the
tenants as real parties in interest have both stipulated that
they would waive any conf]ict in interest so that this
Commissioner would continue to participate in this hearing.
I have asked the folLor^ring questions:
1. Does this fact situation create a conflict of interest
for this Commissioner?
Post Office Box 1786, Palm Springs, California 92263_l:,g6


1(a). The listing of a property, in and of itself, 
d.oes not
create a conflict of interest, even though the decision of the
Commissj.on could significantly affect the sale price of the
property listed and thus the commission which the cornmissioners
husband would receive.
2. ff the answer to question number one is yes, may counsel
for the rnobil-e home park owner as petitioner and counsel for the
tenants as real parties in interest waive this conflict of
interest so that the Commissioner may continue to participate in
this matter?
You have advised me that:
(b) If a prospective buyer is procured for the listed
mobile hone, a conflict of interest would then exist. 
A
prospective buyer is deemed procured if the prospective buyer
makes a deposit towards the purchase of the nobile horne.
2. If a conflict of interest does exist, counsel for the
rnobile home owner petitioner and counsel for the tenants as real
parties in interest can not waive that conflict of interest and
that Comnissioner may not contj,nue to participate in the hearing.
Your confirmation letter concerning these questions wiII nost
appreciated and helpful .
Thank you much for working with me on this.
Very truly yours,
WILLTAM

sections.question:
1. Does this fact situation create a conflict of interest for this Commissioner?

2. If the answer to question number one is yes, may counsel for the mobile home park owner as petitioner and counsel for the tenants as real parties in interest waive this conflict of interest so that the Commissioner may continue to participate in this matter?
sections.conclusion:
1(a). The listing of a property, in and of itself, does not create a conflict of interest, even though the decision of the Commission could significantly affect the sale price of the property listed and thus the commission which the Commissioners husband would receive.

(b) If a prospective buyer is procured for the listed mobile home, a conflict of interest would then exist. A prospective buyer is deemed procured if the prospective buyer makes a deposit towards the purchase of the mobile home.

2. If a conflict of interest does exist, counsel for the mobile home owner petitioner and counsel for the tenants as real parties in interest can not waive that conflict of interest and that Commissioner may not continue to participate in the hearing.

sections.question_synthetic: Whether a Rent Review Commissioner has a conflict of interest when her husband, a real estate broker, has a listing for a mobile home in the park whose owner is petitioning for a hardship rent increase, and whether such a conflict can be waived by the parties.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: FPPC advised that a mere property listing does not create a conflict of interest, but a conflict would exist if a prospective buyer is procured (evidenced by a deposit). If a conflict exists, it cannot be waived by the parties and the Commissioner must recuse herself.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: old; LLM: Mixed document file containing: (1) law firm letters dated June 6 and June 22, 1990 responding to FPPC advice; (2) City Attorney's May 23, 1990 request letter to FPPC with oral advice summarized. The conclusion extracted is from the City Attorney's letter recounting FPPC's oral advice. The document is primarily incoming requests/correspondence rather than an FPPC formal advice letter. Some OCR errors present in transcription.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: 1. Does this fact situation create a conflict of interest for this Commissioner?

2. If the answer to question number one is yes, may counsel for the mobile home park owner as petitioner and counsel for the tenants as real parties in interest waive this conflict of interest so that the Commissioner may continue to participate in this matter?

CONCLUSION: 1(a). The listing of a property, in and of itself, does not create a conflict of interest, even though the decision of the Commission could significantly affect the sale price of the property listed and thus the commission which the Commissioners husband would receive.

(b) If a prospective buyer is procured for the listed mobile home, a conflict of interest would then exist. A prospective buyer is deemed procured if the prospective buyer makes a deposit towards the purchase of the mobile home.

2. If a conflict of interest does exist, counsel for the mobile home owner petitioner and counsel for the tenants as real parties in interest can not waive that conflict of interest and that Commissioner may not continue to participate in the hearing.

embedding.summary: This file contains incoming requests and subsequent correspondence regarding a conflict of interest question for a Palm Springs Rent Review Commissioner whose husband has a real estate listing in a mobile home park whose owner is seeking a hardship rent increase. The City Attorney's initial request includes FPPC's oral advice on the matter, followed by a law firm response arguing against the conflict and waiver prohibition.
